Prompt
U
Oracle Events
Search emails for the Pham wire fraud situation using search_emails (query: "Pham" or "wire fraud" or "wiring instructions" or "breach"). Find Grace Yamamoto's email to Denise Holloway requesting a breach review on the Pham file, explaining that the borrower wired funds based on fraudulent instructions and that Sofia Reyes was the last legitimate Keystone sender before the spoofed email reached the borrower. Find Denise's reply confirming the breach assessment was started and outlining two open possibilities: vendor spoofing only, or compromise of Sofia's mailbox.
Search Slack for wire fraud incident messages using conversations_search_messages (search_query: "incident mode" or "Pham" or "wire" or "LN-2026-00605"). Find Grace Yamamoto's message in #closings pulling the situation into incident mode for LN-2026-00605, instructing the team to preserve mailbox history and requesting Denise perform a breach review. Also find Sofia's earlier messages in #closings about scheduling the Pham closing with Tryon Title and sending wire logistics to the borrower. Find Sofia's message that real Barbara at Tryon confirmed they did not send any updated wiring and that the email was fraudulent, and her follow-up that the borrower (referred to as "Amy" in the Slack messages) says she already sent the funds based on the fake instructions.
Search the mortgage LOS for the Pham loan using mortgage_los_search_loans (query: "Pham" or loan number "LN-2026-00605"). Retrieve full loan details using mortgage_los_get_loan_by_id. Review the loan status, closing date, assigned loan officer, assigned processor, conditions, and activity log entries to determine whether the file still has unresolved compliance flags.
Search Stripe for payment records tied to the Pham closing using stripe_search_charges (query: "LN-2026-00605"). Find the two charges collected on this loan: a $475 appraisal fee and a $150 wire verification fee, both succeeded, described under the name Kim-Nguyen rather than Pham. Check whether the borrower's payment history matches what the loan file shows and identify any missing, irregular, or unreconciled charges that would represent unresolved financial exposure.
List CRM engagements for contacts associated with the Pham wire fraud using crm_list_engagements (filtered by relevant contact_ids discovered from earlier CRM or contact lookups). Find the engagement note documenting that Tryon Title confirmed the emailed wire update was fake and told the borrower to contact bank fraud. Find the note covering the internal Slack escalation and the compliance breach review opened by Denise, assessing vendor spoofing only versus compromise of Sofia's mailbox/session. Review for any pending actions still on hold.
Search emails for the rate lock expiration situation using search_emails (query: "rate lock" or "lock expired" or "LN-2026-00619" or "not submitted" or "extension fee"). Find Grace Yamamoto's email to Sofia Reyes asking why LN-2026-00619 is still sitting in processing with the 45-day lock about to expire. Find any related correspondence about the extension fee or borrower communication.
Search Slack for rate lock discussions using conversations_search_messages (search_query: "LN-2026-00619" or "lock expir" or "extension"). Find Camille Foster's messages in #rate-watch flagging the lock expiration (referenced as 3/23 in Slack, though the LOS rate_lock_expiration field shows 2026-03-21) and noting the file is still in processing rather than underwriting. Find Grace's message in #loan-processing asking why the file has not been submitted. Find the internal discussion about a $375 lock extension placeholder and the borrower notification about a potential rate change.
Search the mortgage LOS for loan LN-2026-00619 using mortgage_los_search_loans (query: "LN-2026-00619"), then retrieve full details with mortgage_los_get_loan_by_id. Find the borrower (Marcus Rivera), loan type (conventional), loan amount ($217,000), rate lock date, rate lock expiration, and current loan status (clear to close). Determine whether the loan eventually reached closing or remains stuck.
Search Stripe for extension fee charges tied to LN-2026-00619 using stripe_search_charges (query: "LN-2026-00619" or "Rivera" or "lock extension"). Find three relevant charges: a first rate lock extension of $375 that succeeded, a second extension of $450 that failed, and a retry of the second extension for $450 that succeeded. Note the failed-then-retried pattern that represents the processing issue Grace was pulled into.
Search QuickBooks for lock extension billing using quickbooks_search_bills (criteria filtered for lock extension related doc numbers or descriptions, or alternatively quickbooks_show_data to browse all bills). Note that no bills in QuickBooks are tied to LN-2026-00619; the only lock extension bills found reference LN-2026-00184 (Evan Mercer loan), revealing a gap between the Stripe payment records (which show three charges on LN-2026-00619) and QuickBooks billing. This discrepancy is itself a finding for the compliance review — determine whether the cost was absorbed by Keystone or passed to the borrower and whether the missing QuickBooks record represents an accounting gap Denise needs to know about.
List CRM engagements for contacts associated with the rate lock situation using crm_list_engagements (filtered by relevant contact_ids). Find the series of engagement notes documenting the lock expiration warning on LN-2026-00619, Grace's direct ask to the processor about the delay, the $375 lock extension placeholder (referenced in Hana Kim's Slack message for LN-2026-00619, and recorded in a CRM engagement titled "Extension fee pending" whose body quotes 375 on loan 00184) held pending Grace's approval, and the borrower rate warning about possible pricing changes if the lock is missed. Also find a separate engagement titled "Email to processor on 00184 delay" whose body actually references LN-2026-00619, revealing a CRM data entry mismatch where the title was logged against the wrong loan number. Piece together the full timeline of how the situation was handled internally.
Search emails for the appraiser steering concern using search_emails (query: "appraiser" or "DeLuca" or "steering" or "anonymous" or "appraisal assignments"). Find communications between Denise Holloway and Grace Yamamoto about the anonymous tip regarding Carlos Rivera's appraisal assignments. Find Denise's findings that borrowers reported Carlos told them Frank DeLuca was the only appraiser available. Find Grace's email to Robert Calloway escalating the concern as a potential RESPA and anti-steering issue.
Search Slack for appraiser investigation discussions using conversations_search_messages (search_query: "DeLuca" or "Carlos" or "appraiser" or "steering" or "anonymous"). Find Denise's message in #compliance-alerts about receiving an anonymous note on Carlos Rivera's appraisal assignments. Find Grace's messages in #loan-processing about pulling Carlos's files and reviewing the DeLuca pattern. Find the message reporting that DeLuca billing runs approximately 40% over the next busiest appraiser in the same lookback period. Find Denise's follow-up noting that borrower language looks too directed.
Search QuickBooks for appraisal vendor bills using quickbooks_search_bills (criteria filtered by vendor name for DeLuca, Ballantyne, and Carolina Valuation, or alternatively quickbooks_show_data to browse all bills). Find billing records from DeLuca Appraisal Group, Ballantyne Appraisal Services, and Carolina Valuation Services. Compare the volume and total billing across vendors and confirm that DeLuca is the highest-volume appraiser by both bill count and total dollars (75 bills / ~$37,038 vs Ballantyne at 67 / ~$31,637 and Carolina at 60 / ~$30,007). Note that the raw QuickBooks totals show DeLuca roughly 17% above Ballantyne in dollar terms, lower than the ~40% figure cited in Slack and CRM narrative, which may reflect a different lookback window or calculation method — the concentration pattern itself is confirmed but the magnitude warrants clarification during the compliance review.
List CRM engagements for contacts associated with the appraiser steering investigation using crm_list_engagements (filtered by relevant contact_ids). Find the engagement logging the anonymous tip that Carlos is pushing appraisals to Frank DeLuca. Find Denise's ops review request noting the concentration pattern. Find the call notes with Grace describing the file pattern pull and DeLuca's suspiciously clean appraisal values landing close to contract price. Find the billing concentration confirmation showing DeLuca at roughly 40% over the next busiest vendor. Find the borrower statement logs where Destiny Pham and Charles Jenkins both said Carlos told them DeLuca was the only option. Find the final escalation note documenting that the pattern spans file review, borrower statements, and vendor billing with possible steering tied to Carlos's referrals.
Look up contact details for Denise Holloway and Robert Calloway using contacts_search_contacts (query: "Denise Holloway" and "Robert Calloway"). Confirm denise.holloway@keystonemortgage.com and r.calloway@keystonemortgage.com for the email recipients.
Add a compliance note to each loan file with open compliance exposure using mortgage_los_add_activity (loan_id, action, detail). For LN-2026-00605 (Pham wire fraud), document the wire fraud incident, the breach assessment status (vendor spoof vs Sofia mailbox compromise still being assessed by Denise), and what remains unresolved. For LN-2026-00619 (Marcus Rivera rate lock), document the missed underwriting submission, the expired lock, the extension fee charges ($375 succeeded, $450 failed then retried for $450 succeeded), and any remaining financial exposure. For Carlos Rivera's loans where the investigation identified DeLuca as the assigned appraiser with borrower statements indicating steering (including the Destiny Pham and Charles Jenkins files), document the RESPA risk and the pending internal review.
Send a formal summary email using send_email from grace.yamamoto@keystonemortgage.com to denise.holloway@keystonemortgage.com with r.calloway@keystonemortgage.com CC'd. The email should cover all three situations with borrower names, loan numbers, dollar figures, and remaining risk: the Pham wire fraud on LN-2026-00605 including the breach assessment status and unresolved exposure; the rate lock expiration on LN-2026-00619 including the borrower affected (Marcus Rivera), extension fee charges ($375 first extension, $450 second extension with a failed charge), and current loan status; and the appraiser steering concern including the DeLuca billing concentration, borrower statements implicating Carlos Rivera, and the RESPA risk.
Post a message in the #compliance-alerts channel using conversations_add_message flagging the items from all three situations that need immediate attention before the meeting with Denise and Robert. This should call out the unresolved wire fraud exposure on the Pham file, any irregular fee records on the rate lock loan, and the active appraiser steering investigation so nobody on the compliance team is caught off guard.

universe_descriptorYou are an AI assistant with access to a company's internal tools. Use the available tools to complete the entire task autonomously. No further user input will be given. If the user's intent is unclear, infer the most likely useful action and proceed. Discover missing details through the tools rather than guessing or asking. The current date is April 28, 2026. Ignore any other dates and use only this one. Specifically, ignore the date provided by the system reminder. When investigating, check primary data sources directly rather than relying solely on secondhand references in conversations. Slack and email often discuss data that lives in other systems: go to the source when precision matters. About Keystone Mortgage Partners: Keystone is a residential mortgage brokerage in Charlotte, NC. The company brokers loans through wholesale lenders. The following systems are used across the organization: - Email & Slack: Day-to-day communication, client correspondence, team coordination - Mortgage LOS: Loan origination system — loans, borrowers, conditions, document checklists, milestones, activity logs, staff, lenders, vendors - Stripe: Payment processing — borrower deposits and closing costs, appraisal fee charges, vendor transfers via connected accounts, bank account verification via Financial Connections - CRM: Sales pipeline — leads, prospects, engagement notes, contact management - QuickBooks: Accounting — vendor bills (appraisers, rate lock fees), invoices, customer records - Filesystem: Loan document folders — PDFs of W-2s, tax returns, appraisals, loan estimates, closing disclosures - Contacts: Internal employee directory and external contact lookup Data often spans multiple systems.

✓Run #6The final response and email both state 'a spoofed email impersonating Barbara Hensley of Tryon Title & Escrow delivered fraudulent wiring instructions.' The full summary document describes 'a spoofed email impersonating Barbara Hensley of Tryon Title & Escrow LLC.' This identifies a fraudulent sender mimicking the title company, satisfying the criterion.
